rules.principle: Deconstruct target job (via TJD or JD analysis) into core requirements before writing or scoring.
rules.why_it_matters: Generic resumes fail database pulls and 6-10s human scans.
rules.detection_hint: Low overlap with target JD requirements; no focused target title.
rules.feedback_template: This resume is not built around the actual requirements of the {{target_role}}.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Map content to top 6-8 JD requirements using similar language.
rules.example_bad: Broad list of everything I've done.
rules.example_good: Experience and bullets aligned to the specific JD priorities.
rules.source_reference: Knock 'em Dead (TJD) + modern JD matching advice

rules.principle: Use Challenge-Action-Result (CAR) for major accomplishments.
rules.why_it_matters: Proves problem-solving and value over duties.
rules.detection_hint: Task-only language no challenge or result.
rules.feedback_template: Rewrite using CAR: Challenge? Action? Result?
rules.rewrite_instruction: Faced [challenge]. [Action]. Delivered [result + metrics].
rules.example_good: Faced stagnant pipeline. Redesigned process. Increased revenue 42%.
rules.source_reference: Résumé Magic + widespread best practice
rules.ui_title: Bullets lack CAR structure (Challenge-Action-Result)
rules.latex_detection_hint: Bullets starting with "Responsible for", "Worked on", or "Helped with" without Challenge or Result.

rules.principle: Quantify achievements with specific numbers, %, scope, before/after wherever possible.
rules.why_it_matters: Metrics increase interview chances ~40%; make impact concrete and credible.
rules.detection_hint: Vague or unquantified claims where scale exists.
rules.feedback_template: Add numbers: $, %, time, people, locations, growth.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Insert truthful metrics or scope (use approximations honestly if needed).
rules.source_reference: Multiple: Resume Magic, Teal, Merit America, recruiter studies
rules.ui_title: Impact is described, not quantified
rules.latex_detection_hint: In experience bullets (\item), look for "Responsible for" or "Developed" without numbers, %, $, or "increased/reduced/cut".

rules.category: Recruiter & hiring manager psychology / scan behavior
rules.principle: Optimize top third of page 1 for 6-8 second initial scan (average ~7.4s per studies).
rules.why_it_matters: 80%+ attention on name, current/previous titles/companies/dates, education.
rules.detection_hint: Key info (title, impact, skills) not immediately visible in top section.
rules.feedback_template: Recruiters scan ~7 seconds. Front-load strongest proof in top third.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Ensure name, current title, top 1-2 quantified impacts, and key skills are in the first visible section.
rules.source_reference: Ladders eye-tracking (2012/2018) + 2025 confirmations, ResumeHeatMap

rules.category: Recruiter & hiring manager psychology / scan behavior
rules.principle: Design for F-pattern scan: prioritize top horizontal, left vertical, specific fixation points (name, current title/company/dates, previous title/company, education) which get ~80% attention.
rules.why_it_matters: Recruiters follow predictable F-pattern per eye-tracking studies (Ladders, Wonsulting 2025); content elsewhere is ignored in initial scan.
rules.detection_hint: Key info not in top-left or fixation areas; dense text in lower/right.
rules.feedback_template: Recruiters scan in F-pattern focusing on top and left. Place strongest elements (name, titles, metrics) in high-attention zones.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Restructure layout: name/top header first, bold titles left-aligned, metrics in first bullets, education lower if experienced.
rules.source_reference: Ladders Eye-Tracking Study, Wonsulting 2025 hidden eye tracker, ResumeHeatMap analysis
rules.ui_title: Key info not in F-pattern scan zones

rules.principle: Aim for 80% of bullet points to include numbers, metrics, or tangible outcomes (per eye-tracking experiments).
rules.why_it_matters: Quantified achievements stand out in scans, increase interview rates (40%+ per studies), and align with recruiter attention on results.
rules.detection_hint: Most bullets are duty descriptions without metrics or results.
rules.feedback_template: 80% of your bullets should have numbers or results to grab attention in the quick scan.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Rewrite bullets to include specific metrics, percentages, or outcomes where possible; use approximations if exact not available.
rules.source_reference: Wonsulting eye-tracker experiment, LinkedIn/TalentWorks studies on quantification impact
rules.ui_title: Aim for 80% of bullet points to include numbers, metrics, or...

rules.principle: Place a dedicated, clustered Skills section near the top (skills-first layout).
rules.why_it_matters: 70-85% employers use skills-based hiring; ATS prioritizes skills over titles.
rules.detection_hint: Skills buried only in bullets or missing prominent section.
rules.feedback_template: Move or add a clear Skills section near the top with role-relevant clusters.
rules.rewrite_instruction: List technical/tools first, then transferable. Mirror JD language naturally.
rules.source_reference: Merit America, NACE, Teal, skills-based hiring stats 2025-2026
rules.ui_title: No dedicated skills section or skills buried
rules.latex_detection_hint: No \section{Skills} near top, or skills only scattered in bullets.

rules.principle: Use single-column, clean layout with standard headings (no tables, text boxes, graphics, columns).
rules.why_it_matters: 98%+ large employers use ATS that parse poorly on complex designs; 97% compliance needed.
rules.detection_hint: Multi-column, tables, sidebars, decorative elements, or image-based PDF.
rules.feedback_template: Simplify to single-column standard format so ATS and humans can parse/read easily.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Convert to clean single column, standard fonts (Arial etc.), text-selectable PDF or .docx.
rules.source_reference: Merit America, Coursera, multiple ATS guides 2026
rules.ui_title: Complex layout breaks ATS parsing
rules.latex_detection_hint: Use of tabular, multicol, or heavy \includegraphics / tikz in the document.

rules.principle: Use keywords naturally in context from JD (semantic matching); avoid stuffing. Include full terms + acronyms.
rules.why_it_matters: Modern AI/ATS uses semantic/contextual matching, not just exact keywords. Stuffing hurts.
rules.detection_hint: Unnatural repetition or missing key JD terms in context.
rules.feedback_template: Mirror JD language naturally in summary, bullets, and skills — context matters more than count.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Extract key phrases from JD and weave them authentically into relevant sections.
rules.source_reference: Tech Resume + multiple AI/ATS 2026 guides (Workable semantic, Pin tools)

rules.principle: Tailor every resume: update summary, reorder skills/bullets, mirror exact JD language for the role.
rules.why_it_matters: Tailored resumes get 78%+ higher response rates and much better ATS scores.
rules.detection_hint: Same generic resume used across dissimilar JDs.
rules.feedback_template: Generic versions get ignored. Customize for this JD's priorities.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Adjust top sections and top bullets to directly align with the specific posting.
rules.source_reference: Coursera, Teal, Novoresume stats, multiple 2025-2026 sources
rules.ui_title: Resume not tailored to this role
rules.latex_detection_hint: Generic summary or bullets that do not mention role-specific keywords from a provided JD.

rules.principle: Lead with a strong professional summary that bridges your background to the target role and highlights top impact.
rules.why_it_matters: First thing read after ATS; sets the narrative and grabs attention immediately.
rules.detection_hint: Weak, generic, or missing summary; starts directly with experience.
rules.feedback_template: Your summary should immediately show relevance and value for this specific role.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Write 3-5 lines naming the role, key strengths/impact, and bridge from past.

rules.ui_title: Weak or missing professional summary
rules.latex_detection_hint: No \begin{abstract} or summary section, or it starts with "Seeking a position".

rules.principle: Emphasize skills and demonstrable competencies over degrees or pedigree; place skills prominently as 70%+ employers use skills-based hiring.

applies_when: most roles, especially non-entry or career changers
rules.detection_hint: Heavy focus on degrees/titles without skills proof; skills section weak or absent.
rules.feedback_template: Shift emphasis to skills and proof of ability – many employers now prioritize this over traditional credentials.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Highlight skills section early with evidence; de-emphasize or contextualize education if not primary.

rules.category: Career gaps, non-linear paths & positive framing
rules.principle: Frame gaps positively as 'Career Pause', 'Professional Development', or 'Family Caregiving' with specific activities (learning, projects, volunteering) during the period; use years-only or hybrid format.
rules.why_it_matters: Gaps are common (layoffs, caregiving); unexplained ones raise doubts, but framed as growth reduce bias and show resilience.
rules.detection_hint: Blank or unexplained gaps; no activities listed during gap.
rules.feedback_template: Address gaps directly by showing what you accomplished or learned during that time – it turns a potential negative into a positive.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Add entry like 'Career Pause | 2023-2024: Completed certifications, volunteered, managed family responsibilities' using action verbs and metrics if possible.
rules.source_reference: Merit America, ResumeOptimizerPro, LinkedIn recruiter posts, Indeed gap guides 2026

rules.category: Non-traditional experience (gigs, bootcamps, self-taught, military, caregiving, side projects, thought leadership)
rules.principle: Translate military experience to civilian terms: use civilian job titles first (e.g., Operations Supervisor (Platoon Sergeant)), avoid acronyms or spell out, quantify scope (personnel, budget, equipment value in $).
rules.why_it_matters: Civilian employers don't understand military jargon; translation shows transferable value and scope.
rules.detection_hint: Military acronyms, ranks as titles without civilian equivalent, no quantification of scope.
rules.feedback_template: Translate your military roles and achievements into civilian language so hiring managers immediately see the value.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Lead with civilian title (Military title in parens). Use civilian verbs. Add scope: 'Managed $X equipment for Y personnel, achieving Z% efficiency.'
rules.source_reference: Hiring Our Heroes, O*NET crosswalk, ResumeOptimizerPro military guide, Indeed military resume tips 2026

rules.principle: Cover letter should add unique value: motivation for this specific role/company, one key achievement with results, how you'll help their goals – not repeat resume. Keep under 250 words, 3-4 paragraphs.
rules.why_it_matters: Adds context (career changes, alignment) that resume can't; shows thinking, personality, fit. Many sources say still worth it if specific.
applies_when: when submitting with resume, especially for roles where alignment matters
rules.detection_hint: Generic template, repeats resume bullets, no specific why this company/role.
rules.feedback_template: Your cover letter should explain why this role now and how your background fits – make it specific to them.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Structure: 1. Why this company/role (research-based). 2. One relevant achievement with metrics. 3. Value you'll bring. 4. Call to action. Tailor per app.

rules.category: Bias reduction, inclusive language & demographic signaling
rules.principle: Use inclusive, neutral language in resume (avoid gendered terms like 'he/she', 'guys'; focus on skills/achievements not personal attributes that could signal demographics). Avoid photos, DOB, marital status, etc.
rules.why_it_matters: Reduces bias in screening (human or AI); blind practices and inclusive language help level playing field. Studies show bias in AI and traditional reviews.
applies_when: all resumes, especially in diverse or regulated industries
rules.detection_hint: Gendered language, personal details (DOB, photo, family), or attributes that could proxy for protected classes.
rules.feedback_template: Use neutral, skills-focused language and omit personal details to minimize bias in review.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Replace any gendered or personal terms with neutral; strip photos/DOB/etc.; focus on accomplishments.
rules.source_reference: Pin inclusive hiring, ACM study on AI bias, SHRM/ Brookings on bias reduction 2025-2026

rules.principle: Use 1 page for <3-5 years relevant experience or new grads. Use 2 pages for most mid/senior professionals (3-15+ yrs). Executives/senior leaders: 2 pages preferred, 3 pages max only for extensive board/publications/scope. Every line must earn its space.
rules.why_it_matters: One-page rule is outdated for experienced candidates. Data shows 2-page resumes often achieve higher interview rates when content is relevant; recruiters prefer seeing full impact without forced cuts. 2026 studies confirm flexibility based on relevance and level.
rules.detection_hint: Experienced pro crammed on 1 page losing key achievements, or bloated 3+ pages with fluff.
rules.feedback_template: Match page count to depth of relevant experience. Trim ruthlessly to strongest proof; expand strategically for senior roles.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Condense early/irrelevant roles to 1-2 lines. For 10+ yrs strong impact: plan for 2 pages. Exec: 2 pages focused last 10-15 yrs + strategic scope.
rules.source_reference: 2026 page length studies (Retuner, Exec Career Brand, ResumeGo, LinkedIn discussions), multiple books (Yate notes level-dependent)

rules.principle: Use standard, widely-recognized section headers: Professional Summary, Skills, Experience / Professional Experience, Projects, Education, Certifications. Avoid creative headers (e.g. 'Where I've Made an Impact', 'My Journey').
rules.why_it_matters: ATS parsers rely on conventional headings to section content. Non-standard headers cause mis-parsing or dropped sections, lowering scores and making human review harder.
rules.detection_hint: Clever, branded, or unusual headings that don't map to standard sections.
rules.feedback_template: Switch to conventional headers that ATS and humans instantly understand.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Replace fancy names: 'Professional Experience', 'Skills & Tools', 'Education'. Keep clean and predictable.
rules.source_reference: Resume Magic, multiple ATS 2026 guides (Workday/Taleo/Greenhouse tested templates), Merit America

rules.principle: Header: Large name, phone, professional email, LinkedIn URL (or portfolio), City/State or 'Open to Remote | Location'. Omit full street address, photo (most cases), pronouns unless relevant, and extra lines.
rules.why_it_matters: Clean header maximizes top-third scan real estate (critical for 7s F-pattern). Extra personal data adds clutter or bias risk; recruiters want quick contact + professional proof points.
rules.detection_hint: Long address block, photo, multiple lines of fluff, missing LinkedIn.
rules.feedback_template: Simplify header to essentials that fit in one scannable line or two. Maximize signal in the first 1-2 inches.
rules.rewrite_instruction: First line: NAME (larger/bold). Second: phone | email | linkedin | github | City, ST or Remote

rules.principle: Structure most bullets as Result + Metric + Context (or TEAL/impact-first variant): Start with the outcome/impact + number, then brief method or scope. Avoid starting with 'Responsible for...' or duties.
rules.why_it_matters: Eye-tracking and recruiter studies show results/metrics at the start of bullets get higher fixation. It flips from 'what I did' to 'what I delivered'. Consistently produces stronger scans.
rules.detection_hint: Duties-first or 'Responsible for' language dominating bullets.
rules.feedback_template: Lead with the result/metric. Use 'Increased X by Y%' or 'Delivered Z resulting in W'.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Rewrite: '[Result + #] by doing [concise action + scope/context].' Example: 'Reduced support tickets 42% by implementing self-service KB and chatbot triage.'
rules.example_good: Cut cloud costs 31% ($187k/yr) by right-sizing instances and introducing spot fleets across 3 environments.

rules.principle: Write bullets as accomplishments and results achieved (what you delivered or improved), not a list of responsibilities or duties performed. Shift language from 'Responsible for...' to 'Delivered...', 'Improved...', 'Led...'.
rules.why_it_matters: Responsibility lists are generic and forgettable. Every candidate 'was responsible for' similar things. Accomplishments differentiate and prove value. This is one of the most repeated core lessons across all major resume books.
rules.detection_hint: Starts with 'Responsible for', 'Duties included', 'Handled', or task lists without outcomes.
rules.feedback_template: Convert duties to accomplishments. What changed because you were there? Add the outcome or scale.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Change 'Responsible for managing 3 client accounts' to 'Managed 3 enterprise accounts; expanded 2 by 28% ARR and reduced churn to <4%'.

rules.principle: Write in implied first person: no 'I', 'me', 'my' anywhere on the resume. Bullets start directly with verb. Summary uses 'I' sparingly or avoids by restructuring.
rules.why_it_matters: First person is redundant (the document is about you) and wastes characters/scan attention. Professional convention across nearly all US corporate resumes is implied first person. 'I' stands out negatively in scans.
rules.detection_hint: Any 'I ', 'My ', 'Me ' in bullets or summary.
rules.feedback_template: Remove all first-person pronouns. Rewrite sentences to start with the action or result.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Change 'I led a team of 5 that delivered...' to 'Led 5-person team delivering...'.

rules.principle: For TJD: collect 5-8 real job postings for the target title. Identify common requirements/skills across them (mark frequency). Build resume Core Competencies + bullets around the highest-frequency items first.
rules.why_it_matters: Single JD tailoring is fragile. Mining multiple postings reveals the true employer language and priorities for the role family. Produces resumes that rank across more postings and feel 'right' to more readers.

rules.rewrite_instruction: Gather JDs. List repeated skills/responsibilities with counts. Prioritize top ones in summary/skills/top bullets.

rules.principle: Cold applications have low yield. Prioritize warm intros, employee referrals, and networking to get your resume in front of decision makers. When submitting cold, make it exceptionally tailored and use LinkedIn to warm the path.
rules.why_it_matters: Resumes sent blind often never get opened by execs/CEOs. Referrals and warm paths dramatically increase response. Books and modern data confirm networking is the highest-leverage job search activity.
rules.detection_hint: Only mass cold applications; no LinkedIn outreach or referral attempts mentioned in process.
rules.feedback_template: Cold resumes have poor odds. Build warm paths and referrals first; use resume as the polished leave-behind.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Resume advice: make it referral-ready (perfectly tailored, clean). Strategy: identify 2nd degree connections, ask for intros before applying.

rules.principle: Use PAR (Problem-Action-Result) or equivalent for accomplishment bullets: identify the problem/challenge faced, the specific action you took, and the measurable result/outcome. This complements CAR/STAR.
rules.why_it_matters: Forces focus on value delivered rather than tasks. PAR is explicitly recommended in major guides as a reliable way to surface accomplishments from daily work. Produces scannable, high-impact bullets.
rules.detection_hint: Bullets describe activities without framing the problem solved or the outcome delivered.
rules.feedback_template: Apply PAR: What was the problem? What did you do? What was the result (with numbers)?
rules.rewrite_instruction: Problem: [context/inefficiency]. Action: [your specific steps/tools]. Result: [quantified improvement].

rules.principle: In 2026, write cover letters selectively (not for every one-click app). When used: 3 short paragraphs. 1) One-sentence alignment to role + strongest match. 2) One concrete proof point with metric tied to job needs. 3) Specific reason for this company + what you'll deliver. Keep <250 words, human voice.
rules.why_it_matters: AI screening makes the cover letter one of the few places for authentic explanation and personality. Generic ones are ignored or hurt; tailored ones add context resumes can't (motivation, gaps, cultural fit) and can differentiate when many apps are AI-generated.
applies_when: roles where alignment, career pivot, or company research matters
when explicitly requested or via non-one-click channels
rules.detection_hint: No cover when it would add value, or generic template that repeats resume without specific proof or why-this-company.
rules.feedback_template: Use cover letters strategically. Make them add unique context + one strong metric proof + company-specific reason.
rules.rewrite_instruction: P1: Targeting the X role because my Y experience directly matches your need for Z. P2: At prior company, [one result with #] that maps to this need. P3: I'm excited about [specific company initiative] because I can bring [your relevant strength].
rules.example_good: I'm applying for the Growth Marketing Manager role because my work scaling paid acquisition at a Series B SaaS maps directly to your 3x pipeline goal. At Acme, I built campaigns that grew MQLs 47% while cutting CAC 22%. Your recent expansion into enterprise verticals is exactly where I can apply similar experimentation at larger scale.

rules.principle: Never include 'References available upon request' on the resume. Prepare a separate, clean one-page references document (3-4 contacts: name, title, company, phone, email, relationship/context) and provide only when requested. For self-employed/gigs, use 'Client references available' or attach recommendation letters/portfolio selectively.
rules.why_it_matters: The phrase wastes prime space and is outdated (employers assume you have them). Separate list keeps resume focused and professional. Protects references until serious interest. Special handling needed for non-traditional paths where verification is harder.
rules.detection_hint: The phrase on resume, or references listed directly on main document.
rules.feedback_template: Remove any references line. Prepare a polished separate list ready to send on request.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Delete the line. Create References.docx with header matching resume and 3-4 entries formatted cleanly. For gigs: highlight client testimonials or 'Strong client references available upon request'.

rules.principle: Keep each bullet to 1-2 lines (ideally 15-30 words). Longer bullets (3+ lines) are skipped in scans. Use concise language; split if needed. Aim for 3-6 bullets on most recent/relevant roles, 1-3 on prior ones.
rules.why_it_matters: Recruiters scan quickly (6-8s). Dense long bullets get ignored. Short, punchy bullets with impact at the start maximize attention and readability for both humans and AI parsers.

rules.rewrite_instruction: Target 15-30 words. Cut filler. One idea per bullet. Example: split a long duty list into focused impact statements.

rules.detection_hint: Single format only, or image-based/scanned PDF, or complex design PDF.

rules.rewrite_instruction: Export clean .docx from Word/Google Docs. For PDF: export from same source as text-selectable, not design tool with graphics. Test parse if possible.

rules.principle: Most recent or most relevant role: 4-6 strong bullets. Previous roles: 2-4. Older or less relevant: 1-2 lines or just title/company/dates. Prioritize quality and recency over exhaustive history.
rules.why_it_matters: Recruiters spend most time on recent/relevant. Overloading old roles or listing too many bullets dilutes signal and creates wall-of-text. Space on 1-2 pages is limited.
rules.detection_hint: Equal bullet count across all jobs, or 8+ bullets on old roles.
rules.feedback_template: Front-load detail on recent/impactful roles. Condense or summarize the rest.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Trim older roles aggressively. Use 4-6 bullets where your story is strongest; 1-2 bullets for supporting history.
rules.source_reference: Tech Resume Inside Out, 2026 length and scan studies (Extern, AIApply, LinkedIn posts)

rules.principle: Use a structured set of 20-50+ targeted 'impact-mining' questions per role to extract accomplishments (problems solved, before/after, metrics in revenue/cost/time/people/quality/scope, recognition, unique contributions). Do not rely on memory or duty lists alone.
rules.why_it_matters: Most people under-report impact. Systematic questioning (as used by professional writers) surfaces quantifiable stories that turn average experience into standout proof. Resume Magic uses 200+ such questions.
rules.detection_hint: Bullets are generic duties; no evidence of systematic extraction process.
rules.feedback_template: Apply impact-mining questions: What problems did you solve? What changed (before/after)? What was the scale and result?
rules.rewrite_instruction: For each role, answer: problems addressed, actions with tools, quantified results ($, %, time, people, scope), recognition received. Convert answers into bullets.
rules.source_reference: Resume Magic (Susan Whitcomb) - 200+ impact-mining questions; cross-referenced with CAR/PAR methods

rules.principle: Pair every strong action verb with a specific accomplishment proof in the same bullet. Maintain or reference categorized verb lists (600+ examples) where each verb is demonstrated with a result-oriented sentence.
rules.why_it_matters: Verbs alone are weak without evidence. Professional resources pair 600+ verbs with example accomplishment statements so writers select verbs that naturally lead to results.
applies_when: writing or editing bullets and summary
rules.detection_hint: Strong verb followed by vague duty or no metric/result.
rules.feedback_template: After the verb, immediately deliver the proof (result + metric).
rules.rewrite_instruction: Replace 'Led team' with 'Led 8-person cross-functional team to deliver X on time, achieving Y% improvement.' Use proven verb + proof pairs.
rules.source_reference: Resume Magic - action verbs with 600+ example accomplishment statements

rules.principle: Select resume format deliberately: Chronological (standard progression), Functional (skill clusters for career changers/heavy gaps), Combination/Hybrid (most versatile), Accomplishments-focused (impact-heavy), Creative (design fields only), Dateless (age bias mitigation). Default to clean reverse-chronological or hybrid for ATS.
rules.why_it_matters: Wrong format hides strengths or triggers ATS/human bias. Resume Magic details a dozen formats with guidance on when each flatters the candidate's situation.
rules.detection_hint: Using pure functional for stable career or chronological for major gaps without reason.
rules.feedback_template: Choose format to highlight your strengths and minimize weaknesses (gaps, career changes, promotions).
rules.rewrite_instruction: For stable recent progression: chronological/hybrid. Career changer or gaps: functional or combination with skills first. Creative roles: limited visual elements only.
rules.source_reference: Resume Magic - detailed chapters on Chronological, Functional, Combination, Accomplishments, Creative, Dateless formats

rules.principle: Follow a deliberate 5-step / RIGHT formula process: Research target + job, Identify transferable value, Generate targeted phrases, Hook with role + fit in opening, Tell story with one proof point + metric, Close with call to action and enthusiasm. Keep to 3-4 paragraphs, <1 page.
rules.why_it_matters: Cover letters succeed when systematic and targeted rather than generic. Phrase Book and guides emphasize structured process (5 steps, RIGHT formula) to ensure every paragraph adds strategic value beyond the resume.
rules.detection_hint: Generic opening, no research, repeats resume bullets, no specific company hook.
rules.feedback_template: Apply structured process: research first, then targeted hook + one strong proof + company-specific close.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Step 1: Research JD + company. Step 2: Map your top proof. Step 3: Draft hook (role + strongest match). Step 4: One metric story. Step 5: Why them + CTA.
rules.source_reference: The Resume and Cover Letter Phrase Book - 5-step process and RIGHT formula for cover letters

rules.why_it_matters: Resumes are searched as keyword indexes by recruiters and ATS. A concentrated, well-organized block improves ranking and quick human relevance checks. Resume Magic explicitly calls for a keyword index representing knowledge base and skill set.
applies_when: most professional resumes, especially technical or keyword-sensitive fields
rules.detection_hint: Skills scattered only in bullets or missing a dedicated top or mid-page block.
rules.feedback_template: Add or strengthen a concentrated keyword/competencies block using exact JD language.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Create 2-3 lines or a table-free list of 10-20 high-value terms grouped (e.g., Technical | Methodologies | Tools | Domains).

rules.principle: Use explicit before-and-after comparisons in bullets or summary where possible (e.g., 'Reduced X from A to B', 'Improved Y by Z% from baseline'). This makes impact concrete and credible.
rules.why_it_matters: Comparisons are powerful communication tools that instantly convey scale and improvement. Resume Magic highlights before-and-after as a key strategy throughout.
applies_when: roles with measurable process, efficiency, or outcome changes
rules.detection_hint: Absolute claims without baseline or change data ('improved customer satisfaction' without numbers or prior state).
rules.feedback_template: Add the 'from-to' or percentage change to make the delta visible.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Rewrite: 'Increased on-time delivery from 68% to 94%' instead of 'Improved delivery performance'.
rules.source_reference: Resume Magic - emphasis on before-and-after stories and comparisons

rules.why_it_matters: Phrase Book and guides catalog dozens of instant disqualifiers. Avoiding them is as important as including strong content.
rules.detection_hint: Clichés, repetition of resume, self-focused language without value to employer, negative tone.
rules.feedback_template: Cut filler and negatives. Every sentence must add unique, positive, targeted value.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Review against known '25 things not to say' lists. Replace generic openers with role + proof hook.

rules.principle: Treat your full online profile (LinkedIn + portfolio + recommendations) as the rich, multimedia primary signal that recruiters actively source from. The traditional resume is secondary 'fine print' or a tailored snapshot for formal applications.
rules.why_it_matters: Recruiters spend more time in LinkedIn and online channels than reading cold resumes. A strong digital presence builds reputation and gets you found; resume alone is often incomplete or distrusted.
rules.detection_hint: Minimal or outdated LinkedIn/profile while relying only on resume submissions.
rules.feedback_template: Build and maintain a rich online presence as your always-on marketing asset. Use resume to support it for specific apps.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Ensure LinkedIn has detailed experience, recommendations, projects, and activity. Keep consistent with resume but expanded.

rules.principle: The resume is not a complete autobiography. It is a deliberately tailored snapshot optimized for a specific target. Omit or condense irrelevant history; focus on what the customer (employer) needs to see for this role.
rules.why_it_matters: Trying to include everything creates unfocused documents that fail both ATS and human scans. Professional advice stresses strategic omission and emphasis.
rules.detection_hint: Long lists of unrelated jobs or skills with equal weight; no clear targeting.
rules.feedback_template: Curate ruthlessly. Every line must earn its place for the target role.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Remove or minimize early/irrelevant experience. Reorder or emphasize sections that map to the JD.

rules.principle: Place Education near top for recent grads/entry-level (<2-3 yrs exp) with full details (degree, school, location, graduation date, GPA if >=3.5, relevant coursework, honors, thesis/projects). For mid/senior (5+ yrs), move after Experience and minimize to degree, institution, year (omit year if 15+ yrs or to avoid age bias).
rules.why_it_matters: Entry-level sells potential via credentials; experienced sells proven results. Wrong placement signals junior or outdated focus. 2026 guidance consistently differentiates by career stage.
rules.detection_hint: Education first for experienced pros, or minimal details for new grads.
rules.feedback_template: Adjust education position and detail depth to your experience level.
rules.rewrite_instruction: New grad: Education top + GPA/coursework/honors. Experienced: After Experience, 'B.S. Computer Science, University Name, 2012'.

rules.why_it_matters: Objectives are outdated and self-focused. Modern summaries act as a branded headline that immediately shows fit and hooks the scanner. Resume Magic contrasts traditional Objective vs. Qualifications Summary.

rules.detection_hint: Starts with 'Seeking a position...' or generic objective.
rules.feedback_template: Replace objective with a summary that sells your qualifications and personal brand for this role.

rules.principle: Prepare a separate one-page references document (3-5 contacts: name, title, company, phone, email, relationship/context, and note that permission was granted). Only mention 'References available upon request' in very limited cases (e.g., self-employed/gaps). Never list references on the main resume.
rules.why_it_matters: Wastes space on resume; employers ask when needed. Separate list is professional and protects contacts. Resume Magic and others note separate lists and limited 'available' phrasing.
rules.detection_hint: References line on resume or no prepared list.
rules.feedback_template: Remove any references mention from resume. Prepare and have ready a clean separate list (get permission first).
rules.rewrite_instruction: Create References page matching resume header. List 3-5 with details. Only note availability for non-traditional paths.

rules.principle: Aim for 1 page if under 8-10 years relevant experience or entry/mid-level with limited high-impact items. Use 2 pages for senior roles (10+ years) with substantial leadership/scope/achievements that justify the space. Never pad; every line must earn its place. Avoid going beyond 2 pages except for very senior/exec with extensive credentials.
rules.why_it_matters: Longer resumes for experienced pros are acceptable if dense with relevant proof; forced 1-page cuts value. Tech Resume and Magic discuss length preferences and 'don't go over unless lots of experience'.
applies_when: deciding final length and content cuts
rules.detection_hint: 1-page cram for senior with 15+ yrs lost achievements, or 3+ page bloat.
rules.feedback_template: Match length to depth of relevant high-impact experience. Trim ruthlessly.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Condense early career. Expand recent senior roles with strategic detail if space warranted.

rules.principle: The candidate's full name must be clearly stated and prominent in the header (largest text, first thing on the page).
rules.why_it_matters: Recruiters and ATS need to immediately know who the document belongs to. Unclear or missing name makes the resume unusable.
rules.detection_hint: Name is missing, uses placeholder, nickname only, or is not the largest/most prominent text.
rules.feedback_template: Clearly state your full name in the header.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Put your legal full name in large bold text at the very top.

rules.principle: The header or top of the resume must explicitly state your current or most recent professional role title.
rules.why_it_matters: The headline is the highest-weight keyword line. A generic or missing title under-ranks you and hides your actual expertise.
rules.detection_hint: No role title in header, or only a very generic 'Software Engineer' when you have platform/specialized experience.
rules.feedback_template: Specify your current or most recent job title explicitly.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Use a specific title that reflects your actual work (e.g. 'Senior Platform Engineer' instead of 'Software Engineer').
rules.source_reference: UI feedback examples + tailoring rules

rules.latex_detection_hint: Look in header or after name for \textit{Software Engineer} or similar. Detect generic titles vs specific platform ones.

rules.principle: Every position in the Experience section must have clear start and end dates (month + year, or 'Present' for current role).
rules.why_it_matters: Dates establish duration and recency of experience. Missing dates make it impossible to evaluate career progression and tenure.
rules.detection_hint: Experience entries lack start/end dates or use vague formats.
rules.feedback_template: Add start and end months and years for each role.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Format as: 'Company — Role | Jan 2022 – Mar 2024' or 'Present' for current.
rules.example_bad: Experience sdf sdf — sdf sdf (f sdf sdf)
rules.example_good: Acme Corp — Senior Platform Engineer | Jan 2022 – Present

rules.latex_detection_hint: In experience section (\section{Experience}), check each \item or role block for dates like Jan 2022 -- Present. Flag missing \textbf{... | Month Year -- Month Year}.

rules.principle: The resume must contain a dedicated, visible Skills or Technologies section that explicitly lists concrete tools, languages, and frameworks.
rules.why_it_matters: 70-85% of employers and ATS prioritize skills. Burying them only in bullets or omitting them entirely makes the resume weak for skills-based filtering.
rules.detection_hint: No Skills section, or skills are only mentioned incidentally inside bullets with no consolidated list.
rules.feedback_template: Add a skills section listing relevant technical skills and tools.
rules.rewrite_instruction: Add a clear 'Skills' or 'Technologies' section near the top or after summary, listing specific items (React, Node.js, AWS, PostgreSQL, etc.).
rules.example_bad: No concrete skills or software tools named
rules.example_good: Skills: React, Node.js, TypeScript, AWS, PostgreSQL, Docker, Kubernetes
